speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, survivor Dolf Ringel, interviewer Bonnie [? Dwork. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked at the consulate until the summer of 1944. D-Day had come. And the British and the Americans were advancing through France. And the-- with this, the flow of refugees from Holland, Belgium, and France had stopped. There was very little to do in France-- in-- in Spain anymore. And Dottie and I had previously applied to be sent to-- to Dutch Foreign Office in London. We wanted to go to England. That had been our plan from the start. The consulate sent a telegram to London, and got a reply that we should immediately come over. They described what I-- we had done, et cetera, languages and abilities. And they wanted us there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Beginning of December, 1944, we departed from Madrid to Lisbon. From Lisbon, we got-- flew by KLM plane to London, taking along the two little sons of the newly appointed Dutch ambassador in liberated Paris, who had asked us to take the kids along. The-- the flight was extremely dangerous because of unexpected storms and ice. We came to London. Dottie and I were separated once more to be debriefed by the British intelligence service. Then we were-- we came to the Dutch Foreign Office. She worked as a Secretary of the Dutch foreign minister and I in the Financial Control Department, checking the expenses that consulates and embassies around the world sent in. The war in Europe ended on May-- officially on May 8, 1945. The department prepared for its return. And we did return in August, 1945 to-- to Holland. In the meantime, in May, I had received a letter-- a telegram from my brother, who was a captain in the British army that he had reached Amsterdam and had just come too late, a week, because my father had died of starvation on April the 14th, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I mourned the death of my father. And in August, we returned to find my mother. She survived just by the skin of her teeth. She was in bad shape when my brother met her. But he was intelligent enough to feed her only food that she was able to take. She survived. They found an apartment of a-- a Dutch Nazi who had fled. And very close to where we used to have-- live before the war. And Dottie and I lived then with my mother. In February, '47, my mother left with my brother, who had left the British Army, for the United States. 19-- February, 1947. Meanwhile, Dottie and I started to rebuilding the business that her father and brother had had before the war, which had been robbed empty except for the walls that were standing, a warehouse, and one of the-- the canals of Amsterdam, in the center of Amsterdam, and two the little houses behind there. It went quite well. And we did well. But then started the Cold War. And '49, it looked as if the United States and Russia would get into another war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had no family in Europe anymore. Dottie had lost both parents and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters, after Franco had won the war, succeeded in 1940 to get visas for the United States. And they and the children all left for the United States. My eldest sister settled with her husband and two daughters in Los Angeles. And my other sister, also two years older than I, she settled with her husband and son in New York, in Washington Heights, where she is still living. Her husband passed away 21 years ago. And her son, my nephew and his wife, lives-- live in Upper Montclair, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother kept on writing from New York that he has established a firm, in-- a-- a wholesale place in stockings. But he needed me. He needed me. Why don't you come? Look, it is so dangerous in Europe again. Do you want to get into another war? And people were-- were talking. A lot of people left-- left Holland. They had had it. So we left. On July, 1949, we left, flew to Amsterdam-- to New York. I found an apartment here in this building, which belonged to [? Gerds. ?] And we lived ever since here. Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my mother was- came from Los Angeles to live in Kew Gardens. And I got her an apartment here. She lived on the other side of the same building, and lived until 1980 and had very happy years here in this house because she felt safe, and had a son and daughter-in-law living here, and the other son and daughter-in-law living two blocks away. We-- we were not very successful in business. But not-- we didn't do badly. In 1963, I said to my brother, I have had it. I don't want anymore. So-- I'm sorry, not 1963, but 1973. And in 1974, I finished. I retired at the age of 63. A few months later, I went to college, to Queens College, started studying history, got my bachelor degree. And then I continued, got a master degree. And after that, I-- I lectured in schools, high schools, junior high schools, churches, synagogues, temples, and B'nai B'rith organizations, for about four or five years, what happened in Western Europe. And then I retired and finished that. By that time, I was late 70s. And I said, it's enough. And now I am looking after financial interests, which we still have in-- in Holland. And other thing, can help my wife and-- and take it easy. What I learned from what I just recounted and from the war, well, two things-- you must have determination, a will to survive. That we had. Unfortunately, most of our friends, who had no knowledge of what Nazis meant, they were duped in going voluntarily when they received a card like I had received, went, and then they went to their death because the Germans with their devilish plans, they were able to hoodwink everyone, everyone. Even the smartest people didn't know what was awaiting them. But we had the determination not to get into the hands of the Germans. And we had also the determination and the will to live. The fact that all the friends who had been sent to Mauthausen were cruelly murdered by the Germans showed us that once we were in the hands of the Nazis, we would not come out alive. And that influenced us to a great extent to take our lives in our own hands and-- and defy the odds, which we are perhaps one in 1,000 to make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other was from an Orthodox Jew, I turned in a almost non-believer, in the sense of what-- visiting the synagogue, et cetera, and praying is concerned. Because we saw innocent people, good people, rabbis, children, young teenagers, friends, all who had done nothing bad in their lives, were sent to their death. So the question always arises, where was God when that happened? Why did God allow this to happen? So my wife and I still feel very strongly Jewish, very strong. We belong-- we belong to-- she belongs to Hadassah. I still belong to the synagogue. We-- we-- we identify with-- with Judaism. We-- we do our best to help Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But the deep-seated religion that I had when I was young is gone. That was the lesson in my life. What I learned is real religion is what I saw people doing in-- in Holland when they risked their own lives to take Jews in, just to fulfill what the Bible and any religion says-- do unto your neighbor what you want your neighbor to do unto you. That is the guidance of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people who took you in, were they alive after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Wellensiek survived the war, a miracle by itself. He was several times close to the-- to the Gestapo, was able to get out. And his friends became later high placed government officials, ministers. He lived to the ripe age of, I think, 94 or 95. We-- we visited each other very often. And in Yad Vashem in Jerusalem, we applied, and he became a Righteous Gentile. There is-- there is a-- a avenue where, on both sides of this avenue in Jerusalem on that Yad Vashem, the museum of the Holocaust, there are stones engraved with the names of those Gentiles who helped Jews, from all kind of European countries. And he is one of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he aware of the fact that he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he had passed away already. His widow-- his widow received the plaque. At the age of 90, he got married again to a young secretary, maybe 35 years younger. And we are in contact with her still. And we are also in contact and friendship with the benefactors of my parents and Dottie, when she-- Rudolph Fuyts and his wife. We visited them regularly. He passed away a few years after the war. And she died maybe 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we want to thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we'll call your daughter, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Judi, our daughter, who is familiar with our story because I wrote a manuscript. And she read the manuscript and has one copy at home. So now--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, I'm Judi Ringel, the daughter of these two wonderful people you have just heard. I'm 40 years old and the mother of two children. One is alive. One has passed away. That is actually the only thing that I can understand in the-- the horrific losses that my parents have suffered through the Holocaust. That is the one thing that can show me what their pain has been like. My parents are fabulous people. What they have gone through, what millions like them have gone through, is something that should never be repeated again in this world. I was fortunate enough to be raised in a wonderful country where all man is created equal, supposedly. There is freedom of speech, freedom of thought. Hitler took a lot of this away when my parents were younger. They knew fear. They knew insecurity. I did not. I went to good schools, as they did. But I was able to further my education at an earlier age because there was no war in my lifetime that disrupted it. I went into the job of my choice. I wasn't pressured into being someone or doing something that I wasn't. This country gave me that freedom. The countries where they came from didn't enjoy such liberties. I'm a manager of a pet store, which is what I've actually always loved to do. I had done other careers and found that the unconditional love given by animals and the people who take care of them is what I actually and truly enjoy doing in my life. And I had the opportunity to do that. I have the opportunity, should I ever decide to do something else with my life, to pursue that. I don't have to go through the turmoil, the horrors, the atrocities that my parents had to endure. I have my parents, God bless it. I hope for many, many more years to come. They're enjoying their granddaughter growing up, going to college, and making a life and a career for herself. I enjoy that. I'm proud to be their daughter. And I always will be. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Thank you. That was lovely, just lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. We have one more picture in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture on the left is my father. It was taken in 1929, in Frankfurt. My mother's picture was taken shortly before she left for the United States in February 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give us their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Max Ringel, my mother's Toni.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toni-- her maiden name was [? Hammesfeld. ?] What you see there is on the right side is a-- what we call it, a reward that I received in 1930 from the Prussian Minister of Education and Culture as a perfect-- a perfect student in high school, signed by the Minister [? Grimmer, ?] who most likely later on became a SS sturmfuhrer and fought against the-- against the-- the Allied and the Russians. It was 1930, as I mentioned before. The left side again is a dedication by the school. At that time, as you may notice, I was called Adolf. Excuse me. The-- my name of birth was actually Abraham. For unknown reason, when I was a little boy, I was start calling Adolf. Later, when I came to Holland, I didn't want to have this hated name, and changed it to the Dutch name, Dolf, by just dropping the A. Later, when I became an American citizen, I took the Dutch name Dolf as my legal name. This document is from 1941, when I start-- started living in a furnished room somewhere else in town because I was afraid that my presence in my parents' apartment would endanger them. So I went to the-- of the Dutch registry office and officially changed my address. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] By the way, it didn't help any. This document is the official notification by the hospital by-- and the military commander of the campo de concentracion to the Spanish Ministry of War that I had been-- I have been released from the campo de concentracion Miranda de Ebro for reasons of being not able to be used in military service. And as I told in my story, this enabled me to get out from the concentration camp on the 26th of March, 1943. Other friends and inmates had to remain until June 1943, when they were all liberated. After I was liberated from the camp, as I told in my story, I was employed by the Dutch Embassy and the Consulate General. And I received a diplomatic passport, which is that smaller one that you see there with my picture on it and an official letter from the Dutch authorities indicating that I was employed by the Dutch Embassy and should-- and represent them vis-a-vis all the Spanish authorities and should get all the cooperation from them they-- that I'm entitled to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where is the medal? The medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you see here is a reception of those people who are called in Holland the England Fathers, those who managed to escape from Holland during Nazi occupation and reaching England and serving the country, either in the military or in any other capacity. And we were-- Dottie and I were amongst those received by Queen Wilhelmina right in-- in the front of these people and honored with a medal that we all of received, the one you see to the right. We-- Dottie is standing about on the third highest row. And I'm two places next to her. We got a speech from the queen. And she-- she shook our hands and thanked us for the services. And we also later on received a-- a cross. It's called the [SPEAKING DUTCH]. That means a-- a cross for the resistance that we offered. Because fleeing from Holland and going to help the government in any capacity was considered by law the same as being an active resistance fighter. A picture of my mother at her 80th birthday in 1900 and-- 1900 and-- let me think, '68. Was her 80th birthday. She lived to the ripe age of 92 in the same building where Dottie and I are living now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toni, as I mentioned before, Toni Ringel. And she is buried in-- here on the island. This is a book published by the French Wiesenthal, I would call him, a French lawyer-- French-Jewish lawyer-- who collected all the names of Jews who were deported from France to Poland and who lost their lives. My in-laws and brother-in-laws, unfortunately, they are in this book. They were deported from the camp Drancy outside Paris to Auschwitz and Jawischowitz. Their names Frits Parfumeur, Elisabeth Parfumeur, van Kollum, Elias Parfumeur, the elder brother, and Henri Parfumeur, the younger brother. None of them survived the war.
